section.title: A New Heart and a New Spirit
section.sectionRationale: This is the Old Testament's clearest statement of the new covenant hope: not for Israel's sake but for His own holy name, profaned among the nations by their exile, God promises to gather His people, sprinkle clean water on them, give them a new heart and a new spirit, and put His own Spirit within them so they can finally keep His statutes from the inside out.
section.thesis: God acts to save His people not because they deserve it but to vindicate His own holy name, promising a cleansing, a new heart, and His own indwelling Spirit that will finally make obedience possible from within, a new covenant fulfilled in Christ's blood and poured out at Pentecost.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Israel's defilement of the land and God's holy name profaned among the nations; the great promise itself, gathering, cleansing, a new heart, a new spirit, and God's own Spirit given; and the land made like the garden of Eden, filled again with flocks of returning people.

units.label: Why God acts: not for your sake
units.note: the exile explained, and God's name profaned among the nations

verses.text: Again the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, when the people of Israel lived in their land, they defiled it by their own ways and deeds. Their behavior before Me was like the uncleanness of a woman's impurity.
So I poured out My wrath upon them because of the blood they had shed on the land, and because they had defiled it with their idols.
I dispersed them among the nations, and they were scattered throughout the lands. I judged them according to their ways and deeds.
And wherever they went among the nations, they profaned My holy name, because it was said of them, ‘These are the people of the LORD, yet they had to leave His land.’
But I had concern for My holy name, which the house of Israel had profaned among the nations to which they had gone.

units.label: A new heart, a new spirit
units.note: cleansing, transformation, and God's own indwelling Spirit

verses.text: Therefore tell the house of Israel that this is what the Lord GOD says: It is not for your sake that I will act, O house of Israel, but for My holy name, which you profaned among the nations to which you went.

I will show the holiness of My great name, which has been profaned among the nations, the name you have profaned among them. Then the nations will know that I am the LORD, declares the Lord GOD, when I show My holiness in you before their eyes.
For I will take you from among the nations and gather you out of all the countries, and I will bring you back into your own land.
I will also sprinkle {{mayim_tehorim|clean water}} on you, and you will be clean. I will cleanse you from all your impurities and all your idols.
I will give you a {{lev_chadash|new heart}} and put a {{ruach_chadashah|new spirit}} within you; I will remove your heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh.
And I will put My Spirit within you and cause you to walk in My statutes and to carefully observe My ordinances.
Then you will live in the land that I gave your forefathers; you will be My people, and I will be your God.
I will save you from all your uncleanness. I will summon the grain and make it plentiful, and I will not bring famine upon you.
I will also make the fruit of the trees and the crops of the field plentiful, so that you will no longer bear reproach among the nations on account of famine.
Then you will remember your evil ways and wicked deeds, and you will loathe yourselves for your iniquities and abominations.
It is not for your sake that I will act, declares the Lord GOD, let it be known to you. Be ashamed and disgraced for your ways, O house of Israel!

units.label: The land like the garden of Eden
units.note: cities rebuilt, flocks of people filling a resettled land

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: On the day I cleanse you from all your iniquities, I will cause the cities to be resettled and the ruins to be rebuilt.
The desolate land will be cultivated instead of lying desolate in the sight of all who pass through.
Then they will say, ‘This land that was desolate has become like the garden of Eden. The cities that were once ruined, desolate, and destroyed are now fortified and inhabited.’
Then the nations around you that remain will know that I, the LORD, have rebuilt what was destroyed, and I have replanted what was desolate. I, the LORD, have spoken, and I will do it.
This is what the Lord GOD says: Once again I will hear the plea of the house of Israel and do for them this: I will multiply their people like a flock.
Like the numerous flocks for sacrifices at Jerusalem during her appointed feasts, so the ruined cities will be filled with flocks of people. Then they will know that I am the LORD.”

mayim_tehorim.gloss: Clean water, evoking the ceremonial washings of the Levitical law applied here to the whole nation's moral defilement, anticipating the washing of regeneration in the New Testament.

lev_chadash.gloss: A new heart, replacing a heart of stone with a heart of flesh, soft and responsive to God rather than dead toward Him. Jesus points Nicodemus back to this promise when He speaks of the new birth, John 3.

ruach_chadashah.gloss: A new spirit. Paired here with God's own Spirit put within His people, this promise anticipates the New Testament gift of the Holy Spirit, who enables obedience from within rather than mere external conformity.

basic.context: Israel defiled its own land with bloodshed and idols, so God scattered them among the nations, where His holy name was profaned rather than honored: 'these are the people of the LORD, yet they had to leave His land.' In response God makes His clearest promise yet: not for Israel's sake but for His own name's sake, He will gather them, cleanse them with water, remove their heart of stone, give them a heart of flesh and a new spirit, and put His own Spirit within them so they finally walk in His statutes.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical promise of return from Babylonian exile, but its language, a new heart, a new spirit, God's own Spirit causing obedience, clearly reaches beyond mere geographic return to a change no resettlement of land could accomplish by itself. The New Testament treats this promise as fulfilled in the new covenant secured by Christ and applied by the Holy Spirit, not merely in the physical return under Ezra and Nehemiah.
basic.originalLanguages: The promise centers on a lev chadash, new heart, and ruach chadashah, new spirit, replacing a heart of stone with a heart of flesh. This is prepared for by sprinkling mayim tehorim, clean water, on the people, cleansing them from impurities and idols. Only after this cleansing and heart transplant does God put 'My Spirit' within them, causing them to walk in His statutes, a decisive shift from external command to internal enabling.
basic.typology: The sprinkling of clean water anticipates the washing of regeneration in the New Testament, Titus 3:5, Hebrews 10:22. The new heart and new spirit are directly identified by Jesus as the new birth necessary to see the kingdom of God, and Nicodemus, a teacher of Israel, is rebuked for not recognizing this very passage, John 3:3-10.
basic.christ: Jesus explicitly builds His teaching on the new birth in John 3 on this passage: when He tells Nicodemus, 'unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God,' He is pointing a teacher of Israel back to exactly this promise of cleansing water and God's own Spirit given. Paul traces his ministry's power directly to what this passage promises, a new covenant 'not in a written code but in the Spirit', 2 Corinthians 3:3-6, secured by Christ's blood, Luke 22:20, and inaugurated at Pentecost, Acts 2.
basic.god: God's motive here is startling and freeing: 'it is not for your sake that I will act... but for My holy name.' His mercy is not first a response to Israel's merit but a vindication of His own character and word before a watching world. Yet that same zeal for His name produces the most personal, intimate promise in the Old Testament, a heart transplant and an indwelling Spirit, showing that God's glory and His people's deepest good are never actually in competition.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 31:31-34 promises the same new covenant, God's law written on the heart. John 3:3-10 shows Jesus rebuking Nicodemus for missing this very passage. 2 Corinthians 3:3-6 contrasts the letter that kills with the Spirit who gives life. Titus 3:5 describes salvation as the washing of regeneration and renewal by the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: This is one of Scripture's clearest new covenant promises: God will act not for Israel's sake but for His own name, cleansing His people, giving them a new heart and His own indwelling Spirit so obedience finally flows from within. Christ secures this covenant by His blood, and the Spirit poured out at Pentecost begins its fulfillment, turning hearts of stone into hearts that love and follow God at last.

context.literary: Moves from explaining the exile as a consequence of Israel's own defilement (verses 16-21) to God's motive for restoration, His own name (verses 22-23), to the fullest statement of the new heart and Spirit promise in the book (verses 24-32), closing with the land's agricultural and demographic renewal (verses 33-38).
context.historical: Addressed to exiles whose very presence outside the land had become an occasion for the nations to mock God's name, this passage reverses that shame entirely, promising that Israel's restoration, not its continued exile, will be what causes the nations to know the LORD.
context.canonical: One of the two clearest Old Testament statements of the new covenant, alongside Jeremiah 31:31-34, directly quoted or echoed in the New Testament's teaching on the new birth, John 3, and the ministry of the Spirit, 2 Corinthians 3, Hebrews 8:10.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To explain the exile as just consequence for Israel's own defilement of the land, to declare that God's coming restoration flows from concern for His own profaned name rather than Israel's merit, and to promise a cleansing and transformation of heart and spirit that will finally enable obedience.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical promise to a specific exiled people; it is not prescribing a ritual. Its enduring, prescriptive truth is that true, lasting obedience requires an inward transformation only God can perform, and that His mercy flows from His own character rather than human merit.

debates.question: Is the promised new heart and new spirit in verse 26 fulfilled entirely at the individual, personal level of the new birth, or does it also carry a corporate, national dimension for Israel not yet fully realized?

views.label: Fulfilled personally in every believer through the new birth
views.case: Most New Testament application (John 3, 2 Corinthians 3, Titus 3) applies this promise directly and fully to individual regeneration in Christ, available to Jew and Gentile alike through faith, with no remaining unfulfilled component.

views.label: A promise with an ongoing corporate dimension for Israel
views.case: Others, noting Romans 11's discussion of a future, corporate turning of Israel to Christ, see this passage's specific address to 'the house of Israel' as pointing also to a future, national fulfillment of the new heart promise for ethnic Israel, alongside its present fulfillment in individual believers.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm that everyone united to Christ by faith already receives this new heart and Spirit now; they differ only on whether the passage's specific address to Israel implies an additional corporate fulfillment still to come.

study.originalLanguages: The promise centers on a lev chadash, new heart, and ruach chadashah, new spirit, replacing a heart of stone with a heart of flesh. This is prepared for by sprinkling mayim tehorim, clean water, on the people, cleansing them from impurities and idols. Only after this cleansing and heart transplant does God put 'My Spirit' within them, causing them to walk in His statutes, a decisive shift from external command to internal enabling.

typology.type: The sprinkling of clean water, Ezekiel 36:25
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the washing of regeneration and renewal by the Holy Spirit, Titus 3:5, and the cleansed conscience of Hebrews 10:22.

typology.type: The new heart and new spirit, with God's own Spirit given, Ezekiel 36:26-27
typology.fulfillment: Identified by Jesus as the new birth by water and the Spirit necessary to enter the kingdom of God, John 3:3-10, and ministered by the Spirit under the new covenant, 2 Corinthians 3:3-6.

study.christ: Jesus explicitly builds His teaching on the new birth in John 3 on this passage: when He tells Nicodemus, 'unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God,' He is pointing a teacher of Israel back to exactly this promise of cleansing water and God's own Spirit given. Paul identifies this new covenant as ministered 'not in a written code but in the Spirit', 2 Corinthians 3:3-6, tracing his ministry's power directly to what this passage promises: God's law no longer merely commanding from outside but the Spirit enabling obedience from within. This promise is secured by Christ's blood, 'this cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in My blood,' Luke 22:20, and inaugurated publicly when the Spirit is poured out at Pentecost, Acts 2.
study.god: God's motive here is startling and freeing: 'it is not for your sake that I will act... but for My holy name.' His mercy is not first a response to Israel's merit but a vindication of His own character and word before a watching world. Yet that same zeal for His name produces the most personal, intimate promise in the Old Testament, a heart transplant and an indwelling Spirit, showing that God's glory and His people's deepest good are never actually in competition.

crossReferences.note: The other great Old Testament new covenant text, promising God's law written on the heart, closely paralleling this passage's promise of a new heart and Spirit.
Jesus rebukes Nicodemus, a teacher of Israel, for not recognizing that the new birth by water and the Spirit was already promised in exactly this passage.
Paul contrasts the old covenant's letter, which kills, with the new covenant of the Spirit, which gives life, tracing his ministry's power to this very promise fulfilled.
Paul describes salvation as 'the washing of regeneration and renewal by the Holy Spirit,' echoing this passage's cleansing water and new spirit given together.

study.oneStory: This is one of Scripture's clearest new covenant promises: God will act not for Israel's sake but for His own name, cleansing His people, giving them a new heart and His own indwelling Spirit so obedience finally flows from within. Christ secures this covenant by His blood, and the Spirit poured out at Pentecost begins its fulfillment, turning hearts of stone into hearts that love and follow God at last.

sermon.title: What Happens in the New Birth? Part 2
sermon.why: Though preached from John 3, Piper explicitly builds this message's central argument on Ezekiel 36:24-28, calling it 'the basis for the rest of this message,' tracing the sprinkled clean water and the new heart and new spirit given here as the Old Testament background to Jesus' words about being born of water and the Spirit, exactly the connection this study draws out.

quiz.q: Why does God say He will act to restore His people, verses 22 and 32?

options.text: Not for their sake, but to vindicate His own holy name, which they had profaned among the nations.
options.note: Both verses state this motive explicitly and repeat it for emphasis.

options.text: Because Israel had finally repented enough to earn restoration.
options.note: The text explicitly denies this: 'It is not for your sake that I will act.'

options.text: Because the surrounding nations demanded Israel's release.
options.note: No such demand from the nations is mentioned; God's motive is His own name, not external pressure.

options.text: Because Israel's suffering had simply run its natural course.
options.note: A specific theological reason is given, God's own name, not merely the passage of time.

quiz.q: What three things does God promise for His people's inner life in verses 25-27?

options.text: Sprinkle clean water on them, give them a new heart and new spirit, and put His own Spirit within them to cause them to walk in His statutes.
options.note: Verses 25-27 state each of these three promises in sequence.

options.text: Give them a new set of written laws to replace the old ones.
options.note: The promise concerns inward transformation and God's own indwelling Spirit, not a replacement legal code.

options.text: Grant them a new king who would enforce obedience by force.
options.note: No king or external enforcement is mentioned; the promise is internal transformation.

options.text: Nothing regarding their inner life; only their land is addressed in this section.
options.note: The inner life is the direct focus of verses 25-27, though land is addressed elsewhere in the passage.

quiz.q: What response does God say His restored people will have toward their own past, verse 31?

options.text: They will remember their evil ways and wicked deeds, and loathe themselves for their iniquities and abominations.
options.note: Verse 31 states this exact response, remembering and grieving over their sin even in restoration.

options.text: They will forget their past sins entirely and never think of them again.
options.note: The opposite is stated: they will remember, not forget.

options.text: They will blame their ancestors rather than take responsibility themselves.
options.note: The response described is personal self-loathing over their own deeds, not blame-shifting to ancestors.

options.text: They will feel no particular response toward their past at all.
options.note: A strong, specific emotional and moral response, self-loathing, is described directly in verse 31.

quiz.q: What will the nations conclude once God rebuilds and replants the land, verse 36?

options.text: That the LORD has rebuilt what was destroyed and replanted what was desolate, and that He has spoken and will do it.
options.note: Verse 36 states this conclusion directly, crediting the LORD with the restoration.

options.text: That Israel's own strength and wisdom restored the land.
options.note: The text credits the LORD explicitly, not Israel's own strength.

options.text: That the restoration was a natural, unremarkable event.
options.note: The restoration is presented as a deliberate divine act meant to be recognized as such by the nations.

options.text: The nations are not said to notice or conclude anything.
options.note: The nations' recognition of the LORD's work is the explicit point of verse 36.

quiz.q: What image does God use in verses 37-38 to describe how numerous His restored people will become?

options.text: As numerous as the flocks of sacrifices filling Jerusalem during her appointed feasts.
options.note: Verse 38 uses exactly this image of festival sacrificial flocks for the promised increase of people.

options.text: As numerous as the stars filling the night sky.
options.note: That image is used of Abraham's offspring in Genesis 15:5, not in this specific passage.

options.text: As numerous as the grains of sand on the seashore.
options.note: That image also belongs to the Abrahamic promise elsewhere, not this specific text.

options.text: No image of numerical increase is given in these verses.
options.note: A specific and vivid image, flocks at Jerusalem's feasts, is given directly in verse 38.

questions: God says He acts 'not for your sake... but for My holy name.' How does it change your view of God's grace toward you to know it flows from His own character rather than from anything you've earned?

The promise moves from external cleansing (clean water) to internal transformation (a new heart) to empowered obedience (God's own Spirit within). Which of these three truths do you most need to remember is already yours in Christ this week?

God's restored people don't forget their past sin; they remember it and are appropriately grieved by it, even while fully forgiven. How do you hold together genuine sorrow over past sin with the freedom of being fully cleansed?

The nations were meant to see God's restoration of Israel and know that He is the LORD. Where in your own life could God's work in you become a testimony others notice, not for your credit but for His name?

Jesus points Nicodemus, an expert in the Scriptures, back to this very passage and finds him unprepared to recognize it. Is there a familiar passage or promise of God's that you suspect you've read past without truly grasping what it offers you?

translationNotes.note: 'Sprinkle clean water' evokes the ceremonial washings of the Levitical law (compare Numbers 19:17-19) applied here to the whole nation's moral and spiritual defilement, not merely ritual impurity.

The Hebrew phrase for 'new heart' here (lev chadash) differs from the 'one heart' (lev echad) promised earlier in Ezekiel 11:19; the two passages use related but distinct wording to describe the same essential transformation.

'Let it be known to you' functions almost as a parenthetical aside, God pausing mid-promise to make sure His hearers do not mistake His mercy for their own merit before He continues describing the restoration.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. Sermon link verified via live web search and fetch this session, Desiring God (John Piper), 'What Happens in the New Birth? Part 2'; though tagged under John 3:1-10, Piper states in the message itself that Ezekiel 36:24-28 is 'the basis for the rest of this message'.
